engineCodes.h2: Porsche Engine Codes & Average Rebuilt Prices (UK Supply)
engineCodes.h3: Compare costs for every major Porsche engine — M96/M97 flat-six, MA1/9A1 turbo V6/V8, and M288 diesel families. All prices are typical UK market ranges for professionally rebuilt engines with warranty. Click "Get Quote" on any engine to receive tailored prices from our network of UK Porsche specialists.

groups.name: M96/M97 Flat-Six Engines
groups.failureNote: M96/M97 flat-six engines are known for IMS bearing degradation and cylinder bore scoring on early 996/997 models. Our rebuilt units include upgraded IMS solutions, Nikasil-lined bores where required, and refreshed timing components to prevent recurrence.

groups.name: MA1/9A1/9A2 Turbo V6 & V8 Engines
groups.failureNote: MA1/9A1 family engines are known for timing chain tensioner wear and direct injection carbon buildup on high-mileage units. Our rebuilt units include upgraded tensioner kits, walnut-blasted intake valves, and recalibrated DFI systems to restore factory performance.

engines.compatibleModels: Cayenne Turbo GT, Panamera Turbo S

groups.name: M288/DFE Diesel V6 Engines
groups.failureNote: M288/DFE diesel engines are known for DPF/EGR clogging and high-pressure fuel pump wear on high-mileage commercial use. Our rebuilt units include cleaned/replaced injectors, updated EGR coolers, and emissions-compliant DPF systems for reliable long-term operation.

groups.name: M529/M48/M44 Legacy & Performance Engines
groups.failureNote: Legacy M44/M48 engines are known for oil consumption and timing chain guide wear on high-mileage examples; M529/M758 turbo units can suffer from turbocharger seal degradation. Our rebuilt units include refreshed oil control rings, upgraded chain guides, and balanced turbo assemblies for sustained performance

commonProblems.h2: Common Porsche Engine Problems — What It Costs to Fix & When Replacement Makes Sense

problems.h4: IMS Bearing Failure – Why Porsche Flat-Six Engines Fail Prematurely
problems.rootCause: The intermediate shaft bearing is lubricated by splash oiling only; early sealed ball bearings can degrade under heat and lateral load, allowing metal debris to circulate and cause catastrophic internal damage .

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Remove transmission, extract original bearing, install upgraded cylindrical roller bearing with thrust control, replace rear main seal | Prevents catastrophic failure if done before symptoms appear; not viable once metallic debris is detected in oil |

| Full engine removal, crankcase split, bearing replacement, thorough inspection of crankshaft/main bearings, oil system flush | Only appropriate if early warning signs (rattle, metallic particles) are caught before lock-up; labour-intensive |

repairOptions.tier: | Full Replacement – Rebuilt M96/M97 Engine with IMS-Proof Design |
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Supply & fit of reconditioned engine with upgraded IMS solution, new liners/pistons if bore scoring present, bench-tested, 12-month warranty | Recommended once failure has occurred or bore scoring is confirmed; restores reliability with engineered upgrades |

problems.recommendation: If you own a 996, 997.1, Boxster or Cayman under 80,000 miles with no symptoms, a preventative IMS upgrade via a certified specialist offers exceptional value and peace of mind . However, if metallic debris is found in the oil filter or you hear bearing rattle, assume internal damage has begun — at that stage, a full rebuilt engine with upgraded components is often more cost-effective than attempting salvage repairs. On a Porsche worth £15,000–£35,000, protecting the asset with a warranty-backed replacement makes financial sense.
problems.cta: → Compare rebuilt M96/M97 engine prices for your 911, Boxster or Cayman

problems.h4: Bore Scoring Repair Cost – The M96/M97 Cylinder Weakness
problems.rootCause: Thin Nikasil cylinder coatings on early water-cooled flat-six engines can wear unevenly under high load or poor oiling, causing piston ring scuffing, oil consumption, and eventual loss of compression .

repairOptions.tier: | Minor – Bore Inspection + Oil System Flush |
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Borescope inspection of all cylinders, oil change with high-zinc flush additive, compression test | Only viable for early-stage scoring with no compression loss; does not repair existing damage |

| Engine removal, block machining, installation of high-strength aluminium alloy wet liners, new pistons/rings, reassembly | Restores compression and oil control; suitable for moderate scoring where crankcase is undamaged |

repairOptions.tier: | Full Replacement – Rebuilt M96/M97 with Closed-Deck Liners |
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Supply & fit of reconditioned engine with closed-deck nickel-silicon plated liners, upgraded pistons, bench-tested, 12-month warranty | Recommended for severe scoring or when crankshaft/main bearings show wear; delivers factory-spec or better longevity |

problems.recommendation: Bore scoring is progressive — once compression loss or heavy oil consumption appears, minor fixes won't restore reliability. For engines with confirmed scoring but otherwise healthy internals, cylinder re-lining by a specialist like Hartech or Tech-9 offers a cost-effective middle ground . However, if the crankcase shows ovality or bearing wear, a full rebuilt engine with upgraded liners is the only route to long-term confidence. Always request a borescope report before committing to any tier.
problems.cta: → Get quotes for rebuilt M96/M97 engines with bore-scoring solutions for your Porsche

problems.h4: V8 Timing Chain Tensioner Failure – Cayenne & Panamera Critical Weakness
problems.affectedModels: Porsche Cayenne (957, 958), Panamera (970) with MA1.02, M48.01 4.8L V8 engines (2007–2016)
problems.rootCause: Hydraulic tensioners on early 4.8L V8s can lose pressure or rattle, allowing chain slack that damages guides, rails, and cam phasers; failure often triggers check-engine lights and metallic rattling on cold start .

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Replace faulty tensioners (often both banks), reset adaptation values, road test | Only viable if caught at first rattle with no chain/guide damage; high risk of recurrence if guides are worn |

repairOptions.tier: | Intermediate – Full Timing Chain Kit Replacement |
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Replace tensioners, chains, guides, rails, cam phasers; engine may stay in car or be partially removed depending on access | Addresses root cause comprehensively; recommended once any guide wear is confirmed via inspection |

repairOptions.tier: | Full Replacement – Rebuilt MA1/M48 V8 with Upgraded Timing Components |
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Supply & fit of reconditioned V8 with revised tensioner design, upgraded chains/guides, bench-tested, 12-month warranty | Recommended if chain skip has caused valve timing errors or piston contact; eliminates recurrence risk |

problems.recommendation: V8 timing chain issues on Cayenne and Panamera are rarely "minor" — by the time rattling is audible, guides are often compromised. If diagnostics confirm only tensioner failure and mileage is under 80k, a specialist-performed tensioner replacement can be cost-effective. However, for higher-mileage examples or any evidence of guide wear, investing in a full chain kit or rebuilt engine with upgraded components delivers far better long-term value and avoids repeat labour costs.
problems.cta: → Compare rebuilt MA1.02/M48 V8 engine prices for your Cayenne or Panamera

problems.affectedModels: Porsche 911 (991.2 Turbo/Turbo S), Panamera Turbo, Cayenne Turbo with 9A1, 9A2 3.0L/3.8L twin-turbo engines (2014–2019)
problems.rootCause: Turbocharger bearings and seals degrade under high heat cycles; oil coking, carbon buildup in DFI systems, or wastegate actuator faults can trigger boost loss, smoke, or catastrophic turbine failure .

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Remove affected turbo, send for specialist rebuild or supply exchange unit, replace oil/feed lines, recalibrate DME | Viable if failure is isolated to one turbo and oil system is clean; requires precise DFI calibration post-fit |

repairOptions.tier: | Intermediate – Twin Turbo Replacement + DFI Service |
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Replace both turbos with remanufactured units, walnut-blast intake valves, replace oil cooler/lines, full adaptation reset | Recommended for high-mileage examples or if oil contamination is suspected; prevents asymmetric wear |

repairOptions.tier: | Full Replacement – Rebuilt 9A1/9A2 with Turbo-Proof Upgrades |
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Supply & fit of reconditioned twin-turbo engine with upgraded turbo seals, revised oiling, bench-tested, 12-month warranty | Recommended after catastrophic turbo failure with debris ingestion; restores factory performance with enhanced durability |

problems.recommendation: Turbo failures on modern Porsche twins are rarely isolated — if one turbo fails, the other is often near end-of-life. For vehicles under 90k miles with clean oil history, a specialist-performed twin turbo replacement with DFI service offers strong value. However, if metallic debris is found in the oil or boost pressure has been erratic, a full rebuilt engine with upgraded turbo components is the only route to guaranteed reliability. Always insist on DME adaptation reset and road-test validation.
problems.cta: → Compare rebuilt 9A1/9A2 twin-turbo engine prices for your 911, Panamera or Cayenne

problems.h4: DFI Carbon Buildup – Intake Valve Restriction on Direct Injection Engines
problems.affectedModels: Porsche 911 (997.2+), Panamera, Cayenne, Macan with DFI engines (M97.21, MA1.01, 9A1, M288) (2009–present)
problems.rootCause: Direct injection fuel sprays only into the combustion chamber, leaving intake valves uncleaned by fuel detergents; carbon accumulates on valve backs, restricting airflow and causing misfires, rough idle, or power loss .

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Intake system treatment using hydrogen injection or chemical solvents to dissolve light carbon deposits | Suitable for early-stage buildup with mild symptoms; does not remove heavy deposits or restore lost compression |

| Remove intake manifold, blast intake valves with crushed walnut media, clean ports, reassemble with new gaskets | Effective for moderate to heavy buildup; restores airflow and performance; recommended every 60k miles as preventative maintenance |

repairOptions.tier: | Full Replacement – Rebuilt DFI Engine with Revised Port Design |
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Supply & fit of reconditioned engine with revised intake port geometry (where applicable), upgraded valves/seals, bench-tested, 12-month warranty | Recommended only if carbon-induced valve damage or compression loss has occurred; otherwise walnut blasting is sufficient |

problems.recommendation: Carbon buildup on DFI engines is predictable and preventable. For vehicles showing mild symptoms (rough idle, slight power loss), a specialist-performed walnut blast offers excellent value and can restore near-factory performance. However, if misfires persist post-cleaning or compression tests reveal valve damage, a rebuilt engine with revised port design may be the only permanent solution. Schedule walnut blasting every 50k–60k miles as preventative maintenance on high-mileage DFI Porsches.
problems.cta: → Get quotes for DFI engine servicing or rebuilt replacements for your Porsche

problems.h4: Excessive Oil Consumption – Piston Ring & Bore Wear on M96/M97 Engines
problems.rootCause: Worn piston rings or scored cylinder bores allow oil to pass into the combustion chamber; exacerbated by short-trip driving, infrequent oil changes, or low-zinc oils that accelerate ring wear .

repairOptions.tier: | Minor – PCV System + Oil Change with High-Zinc Formulation |
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Replace PCV valve/separator, flush oil system, refill with high-zinc performance oil, monitor consumption | Only viable for mild consumption (<1L/1,000 miles) with no bore scoring; does not repair mechanical wear |

| Remove engine, split crankcase, replace piston rings, hone cylinders if within tolerance, reassemble with new gaskets | Suitable for ring wear without significant bore scoring; requires precise machining and assembly expertise |

repairOptions.tier: | Full Replacement – Rebuilt M96/M97 with Upgraded Pistons & Liners |
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Supply & fit of reconditioned engine with upgraded forged pistons, nickel-silicon plated liners, revised ring pack, bench-tested, 12-month warranty | Recommended for severe consumption (>1L/600 miles) or confirmed bore scoring; delivers long-term reliability with engineered upgrades |

problems.recommendation: Oil consumption on M96/M97 engines is often a symptom, not the root cause. If consumption is mild and borescope inspection shows healthy cylinders, a PCV update and high-zinc oil change may suffice. However, if consumption exceeds Porsche's "acceptable" threshold (1L/600 miles) or bore scoring is confirmed, piston ring replacement alone is rarely cost-effective — a full rebuilt engine with upgraded liners and pistons offers better long-term value and warranty protection.
problems.cta: → Compare rebuilt M96/M97 engines with oil-consumption solutions for your Porsche

finalCta.h4: Don't Let Engine Failure Write Off Your Porsche
finalCta.paragraph: Porsche ownership demands proactive maintenance — especially for water-cooled engines with known failure patterns. Whether you're facing IMS bearing warnings, bore scoring diagnostics, or turbo lag on a 9A1, EngineMarket makes it simple to compare prices from trusted UK Porsche specialists who understand these engines inside out. Every engine supplied through our network includes upgraded components addressing the original failure point, bench testing, and a minimum 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty. With nationwide delivery, insured dispatch, and transparent supply-and-fit routing, you can restore your Porsche's performance without the main dealer premium.
finalCta.buttonText: Compare Porsche Engine Replacement Prices Now
finalCta.disclaimer: All prices are indicative UK market ranges based on historical quote data and repair requests from our network of engine specialists. Dealer pricing is estimated based on published main dealer labour rates and genuine OEM parts pricing. Specialist pricing reflects actual quote data from EngineMarket's UK network. Actual costs vary depending on damage severity, vehicle condition, variant, and workshop location within the UK. Use our free comparison tool to get tailored quotes for your specific Porsche model and engine code. Every engine supplied through EngineMarket carries a minimum 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty — always confirm exact terms with your chosen supplier.

engineTypes.h2: Porsche Engine Types – Options, Costs & What to Expect
engineTypes.intro: Every Porsche engine type available through our UK network, with indicative price ranges and specialist recommendations based on your vehicle's age, condition and budget.

types.title: Porsche Reconditioned Engines (Recon)
types.description: A stripped, cleaned and fully inspected Porsche engine where all worn components are replaced with OEM‑spec parts — ideal for addressing common M96/M97 bore scoring or IMS bearing concerns with factory‑level reliability.
types.priceRange: Typical price range: £3,500 – £11,000 (depending on engine code)

types.title: Porsche Rebuilt Engines
types.description: A complete rebuild of your Porsche engine with upgraded components addressing known failure points — such as reinforced timing chain tensioners for MA1 V8s and ceramic IMS bearings for early flat‑six units — delivering longer service life than a standard replacement.

types.title: Used Porsche Engines
types.description: A tested, low‑mileage take‑out Porsche engine from a verified donor vehicle. The most affordable option when budget is the priority, with full history checks and warranty available from trusted UK specialists.

types.title: Porsche Remanufactured Engines
types.description: A factory‑spec, as‑new Porsche engine: every surface is machined, every component is replaced, and the unit is built to meet exact OEM tolerances. The premium choice for 911, Cayenne or Panamera owners wanting zero‑mile confidence.

types.title: Porsche Refurbished Engines
types.description: A lightly serviced, cleaned, and tested Porsche engine that works reliably but hasn't undergone a complete rebuild. Often a pragmatic, fast solution for older 944/968 models or budget‑sensitive replacements where a full recon isn't economical.

types.title: Porsche Supply & Fit Engines
types.description: Your replacement Porsche engine delivered and professionally installed by a UK Porsche specialist. Includes removal of the old unit, fitting, fluids, and a workmanship warranty — removing installation logistics stress for high‑value vehicles.
types.priceRange: Typical added cost: £1,200 – £3,500 on top of engine price
types.cta: Find Porsche engine supply & fit deals

engineTypes.closing: All Porsche engine types include a minimum 12‑month warranty. Prices are typical UK market ranges and depend on exact engine code, supplier, and any additional fitting services. Use our free comparison tool to get a tailored quote for your Porsche model today.

engineSizes.h2: Porsche Engines by Size – Find the Right Displacement for Your Model

items.title: Porsche 3.0L Diesel Engines
items.description: Porsche's sole diesel offering, the 3.0L V6 TDI, powered Cayenne and Panamera SUV/saloon models from 2009 to 2018, delivering strong torque for towing and motorway cruising. While robust when maintained, these engines are prone to DPF clogging, EGR cooler faults, and timing chain tensioner wear on higher-mileage examples — failures that often drive owners toward replacement rather than repair
items.cta: Get quotes for Porsche 3.0L diesel engines

items.title: Porsche 2.0L Petrol Engines
items.description: The entry-point turbocharged flat-four and inline-four petrol engines appear in Macan and 718 ranges from 2016 onward, offering accessible performance with modern direct injection. While generally reliable, carbon buildup on intake valves (DFI systems) and turbocharger seal degradation are the most common failure drivers for replacement demand in this displacement
commonFailurePoints: DFI carbon buildup on intake valves

items.title: Porsche 2.5L Petrol Engines
items.description: Used in performance variants of the 718 lineup and select Macan models, the 2.5L turbo flat-four delivers heightened output with refined balance. Common replacement triggers include turbocharger bearing wear and high-pressure fuel pump failures, particularly on vehicles used for track days or aggressive driving

items.title: Porsche 2.7L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 2.7L flat-six powered early Boxster, Cayman, and base 911 models from the water-cooled era (1997–2008), forming the foundation of Porsche's modern sports car lineage. IMS bearing degradation and bore scoring on Nikasil-lined cylinders remain the dominant failure points that drive replacement demand for this displacement
commonFailurePoints: cylinder bore scoring on Nikasil liners

items.title: Porsche 3.0L Petrol Engines
items.description: Modern twin-turbo 3.0L flat-six and V6 petrol engines feature across 911 (991.2+), Panamera, and Cayenne ranges from 2014 onward, blending efficiency with high output. Replacement demand is most commonly triggered by turbocharger seal failure, DFI carbon buildup, and timing chain tensioner wear on V6 variants

items.title: Porsche 3.2L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 3.2L flat-six served as the mid-range powerplant for 996-generation 911 Carrera and Boxster S models (1997–2005), offering a balance of responsiveness and refinement. Like other M96-family engines, IMS bearing wear and early bore scoring are the primary failure modes that prompt owners to seek replacement units
commonFailurePoints: cylinder bore scoring on early production blocks

items.title: Porsche 3.4L Petrol Engines
items.description: Found in Boxster S, Cayman S, and base 996 Carrera models, the 3.4L flat-six (1997–2008) delivers classic Porsche character with improved torque over smaller displacements. Replacement demand is driven by the same M96-family weaknesses: IMS bearing failure and bore scoring, particularly on pre-2002 engines with early Nikasil coating
commonFailurePoints: Nikasil bore scoring on early production engines

items.title: Porsche 3.6L Petrol Engines
items.description: The iconic 3.6L flat-six powered 996 GT3 (Mezger), 997 Carrera, and early Panamera models, representing the transition from air- to water-cooled Porsche engineering. While the Mezger-derived GT3 units are robust, standard M97 and early DFI variants suffer from bore scoring, IMS bearing wear (pre-2006), and DFI carbon buildup — the three most common triggers for replacement

items.title: Porsche 3.8L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 3.8L flat-six features in 997/991 Carrera S, Turbo, and Panamera S models, delivering heightened performance with refined delivery. Replacement demand is most frequently driven by bore scoring on M97.03 blocks, turbocharger seal failure on twin-turbo variants, and DFI carbon buildup affecting intake valve operation

items.title: Porsche 4.0L Petrol Engines
items.description: Naturally aspirated 4.0L flat-six engines power the 911 GT3, Cayman GT4, and select limited editions, representing Porsche's high-revving, track-focused engineering philosophy. While inherently robust, replacement demand arises from track-use bearing wear, DFI carbon buildup on road-going examples, and occasional crankshaft bearing fatigue on high-mileage track cars
commonFailurePoints: DFI carbon buildup on intake valves

items.title: Porsche 4.5L Petrol Engines
items.description: Early Cayenne Turbo and Panamera Turbo models (2002–2010) used the 4.5L V8, delivering substantial torque for Porsche's first SUV and grand tourer. Timing chain tensioner failure, oil leakage from valve cover gaskets, and turbocharger bearing wear are the most common failure points driving replacement demand for this displacement

items.title: Porsche 4.8L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 4.8L V8 powered later Cayenne, Panamera, and early Macan Turbo models (2007–2016), offering refined power with improved emissions compliance. Replacement demand is most commonly triggered by timing chain tensioner rattle/failure (MA1.02), turbocharger seal degradation on twin-turbo variants, and DFI carbon buildup affecting performance

items.title: Porsche 3.0L Hybrid Engines
items.description: Porsche's plug-in hybrid systems pair a 3.0L V6 petrol engine with electric motor assistance in Panamera and Cayenne E-Hybrid models (2014–present), delivering performance with reduced emissions. Replacement demand is specialist-led and typically triggered by high-voltage battery degradation, electric motor bearing wear, or petrol engine failures compounding hybrid system complexity. System / motor code(s): MA1.01 hybrid variants, 9A2.10 PHEV
items.cta: Enquire about Porsche 3.0L hybrid engine availability

items.title: Porsche Electric Motors
items.description: Porsche's fully electric Taycan range uses dual or single permanent-magnet synchronous motors with 800V architecture, representing a fundamentally different powertrain requiring specialist handling. Motor failure is rare under warranty, but replacement demand arises from inverter faults, thermal management issues, or collision damage requiring complete drive unit replacement. System / motor code(s): Taycan front/rear motor assemblies
items.cta: Enquire about Porsche electric motor repair

engineSizes.closing: Not sure which engine size your Porsche needs? Enter your registration number and we'll match you to the exact engine code and displacement — instantly. All engines supplied with a minimum 12‑month warranty from UK Porsche specialists.

fuelTypes.closing: Not sure which fuel type your Porsche has? Enter your registration number above to instantly identify your model, engine code, fuel system and compatible replacement options – all backed by a minimum 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty from UK Porsche specialists.

engineCodeDirectory.h2: Porsche Engine Codes – Most Replaced Engines (2008–2025)
engineCodeDirectory.intro: Every major Porsche engine code from the last two decades, grouped by family, with compatible models and typical reasons why owners seek replacement. Select your engine to compare rebuilt prices and get quotes from UK Porsche specialists.

entries.title: M96.31 3.6L Petrol
entries.description: Found in 996.1 Carrera models (1999–2004), this early water-cooled flat-six is highly sought for replacement due to intermediate shaft (IMS) bearing collapse and rear main seal degradation. Catastrophic failure usually stems from oil starvation at the bearing, which rapidly destroys camshaft timing alignment. Quality rebuilt units include uprated ceramic IMS solutions, reinforced rear seal housings, and refreshed timing covers to prevent recurrence.
entries.cta: Get quotes for M96.31 Porsche engine replacement

entries.title: M97.01 3.6L Petrol
entries.description: Used across 997.1 Carrera variants (2005–2008), this refined flat-six shares the IMS vulnerability of its predecessor but adds early Direct Fuel Injection (DFI) complications. Owners typically seek replacement after noticing carbon-induced misfires or early bore scoring from extended oil change intervals. Specialist rebuilds address this with reinforced IMS assemblies, upgraded high-pressure injectors, and professionally cleaned intake ports.
entries.cta: Compare M97.01 Porsche engine prices

entries.title: M97.21 3.8L Petrol
entries.description: Powers 997.2 Carrera S and early GT3 derivatives (2009–2012), featuring DFI mapping that improved emissions but accelerated carbon deposit formation on intake valves. Coolant pipe degradation and VarioCam solenoid fatigue are secondary failure triggers driving owners to specialist networks. Rebuilt units arrive with walnut-blasted cylinder heads, reinforced coolant manifolds, and recalibrated valve timing components.
entries.cta: Find rebuilt M97.21 Porsche specialists

entries.title: M96.25 2.7L Petrol
entries.description: Standard fitment for early 986 Boxster base models (1997–2004), sharing the M96 architecture’s susceptibility to IMS failure and Nikasil coating wear. Low-mileage donor engines often fail prematurely due to infrequent oil pressure monitoring in track-driven examples. Certified rebuilt replacements feature dual-row IMS upgrades, honed Nikasil-lined bores, and matched piston rings to restore factory compression.
entries.cta: Request specialist quote for M96.25 Porsche replacement

entries.title: M97.03 3.8L Petrol
entries.description: Fitted to 997.1 Carrera S and 4S platforms (2005–2008), known for timing chain tensioner fatigue and secondary air pump seal leaks under sustained thermal cycling. Owners typically replace units after diagnostic scans reveal persistent fault codes or metallic debris in oil filters. Upgraded rebuilds incorporate reinforced hydraulic tensioners, revised tensioner rails, and new secondary air pump assemblies.
entries.cta: Check M97.03 Porsche engine availability

entries.title: M96.26 2.7L Petrol
entries.description: Installed in early 987 Cayman and Boxster base trims (2006–2008), suffering from similar bore scoring and oil consumption patterns as larger M96 units. High-revving driving habits often accelerate piston ring wear and intermediate shaft degradation beyond economical repair thresholds. Rebuilt specifications include revised piston ring packs, upgraded intermediate shaft bearings, and new oil gallery seals.
entries.cta: Compare M96.26 Porsche engine rebuild costs

entries.title: M96.35 3.6L Petrol
entries.description: The legendary Mezger-derived flat-six found in 996 GT3 and GT3 RS models (1999–2005), prized for dry-sump lubrication but vulnerable to high-RPM bearing fatigue and crankshaft scoring. Track-focused use often leads to oil aeration and subsequent main bearing spin under sustained cornering loads. Specialist rebuilds utilise forged connecting rods, race-spec main bearings, and fully balanced crankshafts for restored track reliability.
entries.cta: Get quotes for M96.35 Mezger replacement

entries.title: MA1.02 4.8L Petrol
entries.description: Powers 958 Cayenne Turbo and 970 Panamera Turbo saloons (2010–2016), heavily targeted for replacement due to hydraulic timing chain tensioner collapse and rapid guide fragmentation. Delayed replacement often results in chain jump, valve-to-piston contact, and complete valvetrain destruction. UK-supplied rebuilt units feature uprated hydraulic tensioner kits, reinforced timing rails, and new chain guides to eliminate rattle permanently.
entries.cta: Compare MA1.02 Porsche V8 engine quotes

entries.title: 948.01 4.8L Petrol
entries.description: Fitted to Cayenne GTS and Panamera GTS trims (2012–2017), sharing the MA1 architecture but tuned for higher naturally aspirated output. Common replacement triggers include excessive oil consumption from worn piston rings and secondary air injection pump failure. Rebuilt configurations include revised oil control rings, upgraded PCV valve assemblies, and new secondary air pump modules.
entries.cta: Find rebuilt 948.01 Porsche engine prices

entries.title: MA1.01 3.6L Petrol
entries.description: Used in base Cayenne S and Panamera S Hybrid platforms (2011–2016), experiencing hybrid coupling wear and oil pressure fluctuation issues under mixed electric/petrol loads. High-mileage commercial SUVs frequently require replacement due to internal bearing wear exacerbated by heavy towing cycles. Specialist rebuilds incorporate reinforced hybrid coupling interfaces, new oil pump assemblies, and upgraded main bearing sets.
entries.cta: Request specialist quote for MA1.01 Porsche replacement

entries.title: M48.01 4.5L Petrol
entries.description: Early 9PA Cayenne Turbo engines (2003–2007), notorious for plastic coolant crossover pipe failure and timing chain slack. Overheating events from cracked pipes often warp cylinder heads, making repair economically unviable. Rebuilt units replace vulnerable coolant passages with machined aluminium alternatives, alongside reinforced timing components and new water pump assemblies.
entries.cta: Check M48.01 Porsche early Cayenne availability

entries.title: 9A1 3.0L Petrol
entries.description: Modern turbocharged flat-six deployed in 991.2 Carrera and 718 platforms (2016–2019), primarily replaced for turbocharger seal degradation and intercooler pipe cracking. Sustained thermal cycling causes rubber coolant and boost hoses to split, leading to sudden power loss. Quality rebuilds include revised turbocharger core assemblies, upgraded silicone intercooler piping, and recalibrated DFI ECU mapping.
entries.cta: Get quotes for 9A1 Porsche twin-turbo replacement

entries.title: 9A2 2.9L Petrol
entries.description: Powers 971 Panamera 4S and 9Y1 Cayenne S (2017–2023), featuring hot-V turbo architecture that suffers from coolant manifold leaks and turbo actuator sticking. High-pressure fuel pump wear and exhaust manifold cracking are secondary failure points driving owner enquiries. Rebuilt units arrive with reinforced coolant manifolds, new turbo actuator mechanisms, and updated high-pressure pump seals.
entries.cta: Compare 9A2 Porsche biturbo engine costs

entries.title: M529.01 3.0L Petrol
entries.description: Base and S variant engine for 95B Macan and 9Y1 Cayenne (2014–2023), commonly replaced due to timing cover gasket failure and turbo wastegate rattle. Oil seepage from the front cover often contaminates serpentine belts, while wastegate play causes persistent boost fluctuations. Specialist rebuilds incorporate reinforced timing cover gaskets, upgraded wastegate linkages, and new oil feed line assemblies.
entries.cta: Find rebuilt M529.01 Porsche engine specialists

entries.title: MA2.06 4.0L Petrol
entries.description: Twin-turbo V8 reserved for 9Y2 Cayenne Turbo GT and 971 Panamera Turbo S (2020–2024), replaced for high-RPM valvetrain fatigue and piston scuffing under extreme thermal loads. Aggressive driving profiles accelerate valve spring fatigue and piston ring land wear in high-boost conditions. Rebuilt specifications include titanium valve retainers, upgraded piston coatings, and reinforced valve spring sets.
entries.cta: Request specialist quote for MA2.06 Porsche replacement

entries.title: M288.30 3.0L Diesel
entries.description: Powers 958 Cayenne Diesel and 970 Panamera Diesel (2011–2018), heavily replaced due to DPF ash saturation, EGR cooler blockage, and high-pressure common rail pump wear. Short-trip urban driving cycles prevent passive regeneration, accelerating soot accumulation and exhaust backpressure. Emissions-compliant rebuilds feature new DPF cores, upgraded EGR coolers, and flow-tested injector assemblies.
entries.cta: Check M288.30 Porsche diesel availability

entries.title: DFE 3.0L Diesel
entries.description: Later evolution fitted to 958.2 Cayenne S Diesel (2014–2017), sharing the M288 architecture but tuned for higher torque output and commercial use. Replacement demand spikes from oil dilution issues, turbo actuator sticking, and crankcase ventilation valve failure. Specialist units include upgraded turbo actuator mechanisms, reinforced piston oil rings, and revised crankcase ventilation systems.
entries.cta: Compare DFE Porsche Cayenne S diesel prices

entries.title: M288.21 3.0L Diesel
entries.description: Early Panamera Diesel application (2011–2014), experiencing high-pressure fuel pump wear and swirl flap mechanism failures at high mileage. Fuel contamination from degraded pump internals often damages downstream injectors, requiring full fuel system overhaul. Rebuilt replacements incorporate revised HPFP internals, blocked swirl flap assemblies, and new fuel rail pressure seals.
entries.cta: Get quotes for M288.21 Porsche diesel engine

entries.title: M758.01 2.5L Petrol
entries.description: Powers 982 718 Boxster/Cayman GTS 4.0 and Spyder variants (2018–2024), replaced for oil pan baffle wear, turbo oil feed restriction, and severe carbon intake buildup. High-G cornering drains oil from pickup tubes under track conditions, while DFI mapping accelerates valve deposits. Certified rebuilds feature reinforced oil pan baffles, upgraded turbo oil feed lines, and walnut-blasted intake ports.
entries.cta: Compare M758.01 Porsche 718 engine costs

entries.title: M44.41 2.5L Petrol
entries.description: Classic 16-valve flat-four from 89/944 and 944 S models (1985–1991), frequently replaced due to oil pump drive gear wear, timing belt stretch, and harmonic dampener failure. Age-related rubber degradation and infrequent oil changes lead to catastrophic oil starvation under load. Heritage rebuilds utilise uprated oil pump drives, new timing belt assemblies, and balanced harmonic dampeners to preserve provenance. Closing line: Can’t find your engine code listed above? Use our full Porsche engine code directory below — every code from the last 70+ years — or enter your registration number for an instant match to the correct replacement engine with warranty.
entries.cta: Request specialist quote for M44.41 Porsche replacement

engineYears.h2: Porsche Engines by Year – What Was Fitted & When

years.preview: M96 Water-Cooled Transition: 996, Boxster 986 & IMS Era Begins Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: 1998 marked Porsche's most controversial engineering transition in decades - the air-cooled flat-six was retired across the mainstream range and replaced by the water-cooled M96 family, introducing the IMS (intermediate shaft) bearing issue that would define this generation's aftermarket for the next 25 years.
keyChanges: M96 3.4-litre flat-six introduced in 911 996 (1998) - water-cooled, replacing the air-cooled 993 unit
Boxster 986 2.5 and 2.7 M96 variants carry the same intermediate shaft architecture - dual-row IMS bearing in 1997–2000 production
IMS dual-row bearing (1997–2000) considered relatively stable - single-row bearing introduced from 2001 represents the highest-failure-rate variant
Rear main seal (RMS) oil leaks are an additional known fault across M96 production - often discovered during IMS investigation
911 Turbo (996) and GT3/GT2 retain Mezger flat-six architecture - no IMS vulnerability in these variants
knownFor: IMS dual-row bearing (pre-2001) is considered lower risk than later units but preventative replacement is still strongly recommended by UK specialists - metal debris in oil on inspection is the primary early warning signal

M96 RMS (rear main seal) oil weep is the single most common service item across all 996 and 986 examples - not catastrophic in itself but masks IMS failure symptoms and should always trigger investigation
996 Carrera introduced cooling via twin front-mounted radiators - inlets can clog with UK autumn leaf debris causing overheating; cooling fan condition is a mandatory pre-purchase check

Air-cooled purists' initial rejection of the 996 has been revised - M96 (when properly maintained or IMS-retrofitted) is now embraced as an accessible entry into Porsche ownership with a strong UK rebuilder community
years.ticker: Common Replacement Enquiries: Porsche 996 engine replacement UK - Porsche M96 reconditioned engine - Porsche 911 IMS failure engine for sale - Porsche Boxster 986 engine replacement UK - Porsche 996 Carrera engine supply fit
years.cta: Search for 1998–2001 Porsche M96 replacement and rebuilt engines from UK Porsche specialists

years.preview: M96 Single-Row IMS, 997 Preparation & Cayenne V8 Launch Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: The 2001–2005 production window of the M96 with single-row IMS bearing represents the highest-risk era for Porsche flat-six engine failure - while simultaneously Porsche introduced the V8 Cayenne in the UK, bringing an entirely new engine family to the brand's range for the first time in decades.
keyChanges: M96 single-row IMS bearing introduced from 2001 production year - significantly higher failure rate than dual-row predecessor
Cayenne (955) launched in UK (2002–2003) with 3.2 V6 M02.2Y and 4.5 V8 M48 variants - first Porsche SUV
Cayenne Turbo arrives with 4.5 V8 twin-turbo M48/50 - 450 PS and 620 Nm
4.5 V8 M48 suffers cylinder bore wear due to Nikasil-style coating degradation - documented in early 955 production
Boxster 986 and 996 continue on M96 single-row IMS platform - risk heightened on examples seeing short-journey use
mainEngines: M96/03 - 3.6 flat-six (996 Carrera S - single-row IMS)
knownFor: M96 single-row IMS (2001–2005) is the definitive high-risk period - UK specialist consensus recommends IMS retrofit as an immediate priority on any purchase from this era regardless of mileage

Cayenne 4.5 V8 (M48) cylinder bore coating degradation causes oil burning and white smoke - repair requires full engine strip, cylinder re-lining, or complete exchange unit; common on 955 examples above 80,000 miles
Cayenne 3.2 V6 is robust for day-to-day use but coolant pump failure is a known and recurring fault - requires monitoring from 60,000 km

996 Carrera despite IMS stigma delivers a genuinely rewarding driving experience - engines that have received the LN Engineering IMS retrofit and regular short-interval oil changes are considered as reliable as any sports car unit
years.ticker: Common Replacement Enquiries: Porsche 996 M96 engine replacement UK - Porsche 911 IMS bearing engine failure - Porsche Cayenne 4.5 V8 engine for sale UK - Porsche Cayenne S reconditioned engine - Porsche Boxster S engine supply fit
years.cta: Find 2002–2004 Porsche M96 and Cayenne V8 replacement engines from UK Porsche rebuilders

years.preview: 997 Gen 1, Cayman S, M97 & Bore Scoring Emergence Major Reliability Revision
years.description: The 997 generation delivered a return to classic 911 proportions and introduced the M97 engine, addressing some M96 shortcomings - but bore scoring on the 3.8 Carrera S and continued IMS vulnerability on early 997 3.6 units introduced new failure patterns that define this generation's used-car landscape.
keyChanges: 997 Carrera (3.6) and Carrera S (3.8) launched - M97/01 and M97/05 engines; IMS updated to larger bearing but still a risk on 997.1 3.6 pre-2006 production
Cayman and Cayman S (987) introduced - M97/21 (2.7) and M97/22 (3.4) variants; same IMS architecture applies pre-engine-number 61504715
997 Carrera S 3.8 M97/05 subject to bore scoring - cylinder liner wear causes oil consumption and smoking; most common on examples used frequently from cold or under-serviced
Cayenne 957 (2007 onward) updated - 3.0 V6 diesel introduced as first diesel Cayenne in UK (Touareg V6 TDI-derived architecture)
Boxster 987 arrives with 2.7 M97/21 and 3.2 M97/22 - IMS exposure continues pre-engine-number threshold
mainEngines: M97/01 - 3.6 flat-six (997 Carrera, Boxster S 987 - IMS updated)
M97/05 - 3.8 flat-six (997 Carrera S - bore scoring risk)
knownFor: 997.1 bore scoring (M97/05 3.8 S) is the most costly engine failure pattern from this era - replacement cylinder liners and pistons are required; UK specialists recommend a full rebuild at the same time
IMS risk on 997.1 is significantly lower than 996 - only the pre-2006 3.6 Carrera (early single production year) carries meaningful concern; Turbo, GT2, and GT3 use the IMS-free Mezger engine
Cayenne 4.8 V8 is a compelling grand-touring engine - chain tensioner and oil separator failures are the primary maintenance items above 100,000 miles
Cayenne Diesel 3.0 TDI (Audi-sourced) is one of the most practical Cayenne powertrain choices - EGR and DPF faults mirror Audi Q7 3.0 TDI of the same period
years.ticker: Common Replacement Enquiries: Porsche 997 Carrera S engine replacement UK - Porsche M97 bore scoring engine for sale - Porsche Cayman S engine supply UK - Porsche 997 3.8 reconditioned engine - Porsche Cayenne S V8 engine replacement UK
years.cta: Compare 2005–2008 Porsche 997 and Cayenne M97 replacement engine quotes from UK Porsche specialists

years.preview: 997.2 PDK, Direct Injection MA1 & Panamera Launch Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: The 997.2 generation introduced direct injection to the 911 flat-six via the new MA1 engine family and simultaneously launched the Panamera as Porsche's first four-door grand tourer - bringing a 4.8-litre naturally aspirated and turbocharged V8 range to UK buyers.
keyChanges: MA1/01 and MA1/03 direct injection flat-six introduced in 997.2 Carrera (3.6) and Carrera S (3.8) - replaces M97, resolving bore scoring and IMS concerns permanently
997.2 Turbo retains Mezger-derived unit upgraded with twin-scroll turbos - 500 PS from 3.8 litres
Panamera (970) launched in UK with 3.6 V6 (M46/01), 4.8 V8 (M46/20), and 4.8 Turbo (M46/50) - all-new platform
Panamera S Hybrid introduced - first Porsche hybrid in UK, 3.0 supercharged V6 with electric motor
Cayenne 958 (2010) updated - 3.6 V6, 4.8 V8 and Turbo, plus 3.0 TDI diesel continue on revised platform
mainEngines: MA1/03 - 3.8 direct-injection flat-six (997.2 Carrera S, Cayman S 987.2)
M46/20 - 4.8 NA V8 (Panamera S, Cayenne S 958)
M46/50 - 4.8 twin-turbo V8 (Panamera Turbo, Cayenne Turbo 958)
CJGA - 3.0 supercharged V6 hybrid (Panamera S Hybrid)
knownFor: MA1 direct injection flat-six resolves the IMS and bore scoring issues that defined the prior generation - MA1-powered cars are regarded as the most mechanically resolved modern flat-six Porsches in the UK specialist community

DFI (direct fuel injection) on MA1 creates carbon buildup on intake valves - walnut blasting at 40,000–60,000 mile intervals is recommended by UK Porsche specialists and adds a routine maintenance cost
Panamera 4.8 V8 is luxuriously powerful but oil separator failure and coolant hose deterioration are the primary fault patterns above 80,000 miles
Cayenne 958 3.0 TDI continues the Audi-sourced diesel - proven and practical, but AdBlue system faults and injector wear are common from 80,000 miles
years.ticker: Common Replacement Enquiries: Porsche 997.2 Carrera engine replacement UK - Porsche Panamera 4.8 V8 engine for sale - Porsche 911 997 direct injection engine supply UK - Porsche Cayenne 958 V8 reconditioned engine - Porsche 997.2 turbo engine replacement UK
years.cta: Find 2009–2012 Porsche 997.2 and Panamera replacement engines from UK Porsche engine specialists

years.preview: 991.1 9A1 Engine, 981 Boxster/Cayman & Macan Launch Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: The 991 generation introduced the all-new 9A1 engine family - a lighter, lower-friction flat-six architecture that achieved class-leading efficiency - while the new 981 Boxster and Cayman deployed the closely related 9A1-based units and the Macan SUV arrived with Audi-sourced turbocharged four and six-cylinder engines.
keyChanges: 9A1 flat-six introduced in 991.1 Carrera (3.4) and Carrera S (3.8) - new architecture, reduced mass, lower internal friction, improved efficiency by up to 16% over MA1
981 Boxster (2.7) and Boxster S / Cayman S (3.4) use closely related 9A1-derived units - IMS and bore scoring issues do not transfer to this generation
Macan launched in UK (2014) with 2.0 TFSI turbo petrol (EA888), 3.0 V6 biturbo, and 3.6 V6 biturbo - all Volkswagen Group-sourced engines
Macan S / GTS / Turbo 3.0 and 3.6 V6 subject to bore scoring - same Nikasil cylinder liner issue seen in Audi-based V6 units
991 GT3 (2013) departs from 9A1 - uses 9A1 block but naturally aspirated 3.8 GT high-revving unit
mainEngines: 9A1 (MCX.SA) - 3.8 flat-six (991.1 Carrera S, 981 Boxster S, 981 Cayman S)
knownFor: 9A1 flat-six is broadly reliable - crank sensor and coil pack failures are the primary fault items, causing intermittent misfires or cut-out at low RPM; typically a £200–500 repair

Macan 3.0 and 3.6 V6 biturbo bore scoring is the most significant powertrain issue in this era - cylinder liner wear causes oil consumption and smoking; specialist rebuilders recommend a full engine exchange on affected units
Macan 2.0 TFSI timing chain stretch is a known EA888 fault - tensioner wear from below 60,000 miles has been reported and requires prompt attention to avoid catastrophic failure

991.1 GT3 initial production (2013) subject to a worldwide engine recall due to connecting rod bearing failure - Porsche replaced affected engines under warranty; pre-purchase inspection and engine replacement history is mandatory on any used GT3 from this period
years.ticker: Common Replacement Enquiries: Porsche Macan 3.0 V6 engine replacement UK - Porsche Macan bore scoring engine for sale - Porsche 991 Carrera engine supply fit UK - Porsche Cayman 981 engine replacement - Porsche Macan Turbo reconditioned engine UK
years.cta: Get quotes for 2012–2016 Porsche 991 and Macan replacement engines from UK specialists

years.preview: 991.2 Turbo Flat-Six, 718 Flat-Four & E-Hybrid Arrival Technology Introduction
years.description: The 991.2 generation was the most contentious 911 update in modern Porsche history - the naturally aspirated 3.4 and 3.8 flat-six were replaced by turbocharged 3.0-litre units across the Carrera range, while the 718 Boxster and Cayman controversially replaced their flat-six with a flat-four Skyactiv-inspired turbocharged unit.
keyChanges: 991.2 Carrera and Carrera S - both adopt 3.0 biturbo flat-six (9A2 / MA176 family) replacing the naturally aspirated 3.4 and 3.8
718 Boxster and Cayman introduce 2.0 (MA102) and 2.5 (MA176) turbocharged flat-four - first flat-four in a mainstream Porsche since the 924; enthusiast reaction divided
Panamera (971) launched - 2.9 V6 biturbo and 4.0 V8 biturbo family; first full Panamera 4 E-Hybrid with 17.9 kWh battery
Cayenne (9YA) redesign - 3.0 V6 turbo, 2.9 V6 biturbo, 4.0 V8 biturbo architecture across S, GTS, Turbo
Porsche launches E-Hybrid technology across Cayenne, Panamera - 3.0 V6 + electric motor combinations
mainEngines: 9A2 (DCK.A) - 3.0 biturbo flat-six 420 PS (991.2 Carrera S, GTS)
MA102 - 2.0 flat-four turbo 300 PS (718 Boxster, 718 Cayman)
MA176 - 2.5 flat-four turbo 350 PS (718 Boxster S, 718 Cayman S)
MCW.JA - 4.0 V8 biturbo (Panamera Turbo, Cayenne Turbo)
CJGA (updated) - 3.0 V6 + electric motor (Cayenne E-Hybrid, Panamera 4 E-Hybrid)
knownFor: 991.2 3.0 biturbo flat-six is widely regarded as more capable than the naturally aspirated unit on performance metrics but purists lament the loss of the high-revving character that defined prior Carrera engines

718 flat-four is technically accomplished - IHI single scroll turbo and high 12.5:1 compression ratio; however, carbon buildup and turbo heat soak at sustained high load have been observed on early 2.0 MA102 units
Panamera 971 2.9 V6 biturbo delivers an excellent balance of performance and efficiency - oil separator and wastegate actuator wear are the primary service items above 60,000 miles

E-Hybrid systems on Cayenne and Panamera are technically impressive - high-voltage battery degradation and charging system faults are emerging watchpoints on examples beyond 60,000 miles and five years of age
years.ticker: Common Replacement Enquiries: Porsche 911 991.2 engine replacement UK - Porsche 718 Cayman engine for sale UK - Porsche 718 Boxster reconditioned engine - Porsche Panamera 4.0 V8 engine supply UK - Porsche Cayenne E-Hybrid engine replacement
years.cta: Find 2016–2019 Porsche 991.2, 718, and Panamera replacement engines from UK Porsche specialists

years.preview: 992 Generation, GT4 Flat-Six, Taycan Electric & 9A3 Evolution Performance Landmark
years.description: The 992 Carrera introduced the 9A3 evolution of the biturbo flat-six alongside MHEV-ready electrical architecture, the GT range returned to naturally aspirated flat-six purity in the GT3/GT4, and the Taycan became Porsche's first fully electric production vehicle in the UK - representing the brand's most diverse powertrain portfolio in its 75-year history.
keyChanges: 992 Carrera launches with 9A3 3.0 biturbo flat-six - revised internals, improved thermal management, VarioCam Plus on both banks
992 GT3 / GT3 RS returns to naturally aspirated flat-six (9A2 GT variant) - 510 PS from 4.0 litres, 9,000 rpm redline; celebrates engineering purity
Taycan launched in UK (2019) - permanently synchronised motors front and rear, 800V architecture, Performance Battery Plus to 570 PS in Turbo S
718 T/GT4/GTS given 4.0 naturally aspirated flat-six (9A2 GT-derived) from 2020 - fan community response overwhelmingly positive over the flat-four
Cayenne 9YB (2023 facelift) - 3.0 turbo V6 plug-in hybrid with 25.9 kWh battery, 70-mile EV range
mainEngines: 9A3 (DPH.B / DPH.C) - 3.0 biturbo flat-six 385/450/480 PS (992 Carrera / S / GTS)
9A2 GT - 4.0 NA flat-six 510 PS (992 GT3, 992 GT3 RS, 718 GT4 RS)
Taycan front/rear motors - electric dual-motor (Taycan, Taycan 4S, Turbo, Turbo S)
MCW.JA updated - 4.0 V8 biturbo (Cayenne Turbo GT, Panamera Turbo S)
3.0 V6 + 25.9 kWh battery - PHEV (Cayenne E-Hybrid 9YB, Panamera 4 E-Hybrid 971)
knownFor: 992 GT3 naturally aspirated 4.0 flat-six is universally celebrated as one of the greatest engines in production - 9,000 rpm, pure mechanical response, and exceptional longevity under track use when fluids are maintained on shortened intervals

9A3 biturbo unit in the 992 Carrera is highly polished and reliable - early examples show no systemic failure patterns; valve cleaning from carbon buildup remains a periodic requirement at 40,000-mile intervals as inherited from MA1/9A1/9A2 generations

Taycan 800V drivetrain is the most technically sophisticated powertrain Porsche has offered - battery thermal management is robust but independent out-of-warranty repair costs for inverter or motor failure are very high; Porsche's extended battery warranty provides some comfort for UK buyers

PHEV Cayenne and Panamera high-voltage components require specialist tooling for diagnosis and repair - increasing numbers of UK independent Porsche specialists are investing in EV training and equipment to service this growing fleet
years.ticker: Common Replacement Enquiries: Porsche 992 GT3 engine supply UK - Porsche Taycan motor replacement UK - Porsche 718 GT4 engine for sale UK - Porsche 992 Carrera reconditioned engine - Porsche Cayenne E-Hybrid engine replacement UK
years.cta: Get quotes for 2019–2025 Porsche 992, GT3, and Taycan drivetrain replacement from UK Porsche specialists

faq.h2: Porsche Engine Replacement – Frequently Asked Questions
faq.intro: Quick, honest answers to the most common questions Porsche owners ask before comparing engine replacement prices and finding a UK specialist.

items.question: How much does a Porsche engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: A reconditioned Porsche engine typically costs £6,000–£15,000 for M96/M97 flat-six units, while high-performance 9A1 twin-turbo or MA1.02 V8 replacements range from £12,000–£25,000+. Supply-and-fit labour adds £800–£2,500 depending on model complexity, with main dealers charging 30–50% more than independent specialists. We always recommend comparing at least three quotes and confirming whether VAT, delivery, and old engine disposal are included. Compare Porsche engine replacement prices now to find the best value from vetted UK specialists.

items.question: What affects the cost of a Porsche engine replacement?
items.answer: Four key factors drive Porsche engine replacement cost: displacement and engine code (2.0L four-cylinder vs 4.8L V8), condition (used vs reconditioned vs rebuilt), vehicle complexity (911 vs Cayenne accessibility), and whether you choose supply-only or supply-and-fit. London and South East labour rates can be 20–30% higher than regional specialists. Our comparison tool provides transparent, like-for-like quotes so you see exactly what's included. Get quotes from UK Porsche engine specialists to compare supply-and-fit deals in your area.

items.question: Is it worth replacing the engine in my Porsche?
items.answer: In most cases, yes — especially if the chassis, gearbox, and interior remain in good condition. A rebuilt Porsche engine with upgraded IMS bearings or reinforced timing components often outlasts the original unit, preserving your vehicle's value far better than selling part-exchange. If your 911, Cayman or Cayenne holds strong residual value, engine replacement is almost always the smarter financial decision. Find reconditioned Porsche engines with comprehensive warranty cover to protect your investment long-term.
items.cta: Get a Porsche engine quote now

items.question: What's included in a Porsche engine supply and fit service?
items.answer: A full Porsche engine supply and fit service covers sourcing the correct engine code, professional removal of the failed unit, installation of the replacement, fresh fluids and filters, a workmanship warranty, safe disposal of the old engine, and a post-fit test drive. Fitting costs typically range from £800 for a 718 Boxster to £2,500 for a Cayenne V8 due to accessibility differences. Always confirm whether the quote includes VAT and any ancillary parts like mounts or hoses. Compare Porsche engine supply and fit deals to secure a complete, hassle-free solution.
items.cta: Get a Porsche engine quote now

items.question: How long does a Porsche engine replacement take?
items.answer: Most common Porsche models like the 911, Boxster or Cayman take 3–5 working days for a like-for-like engine swap. More complex vehicles such as the Cayenne or Panamera with V8 architectures may require 5–7 days due to tighter engine bay access. The biggest variable is engine availability — our network stocks commonly replaced codes like M96, MA1.02 and 9A1 to minimise downtime. Use our free comparison tool to ask suppliers about current lead times for your specific Porsche engine code.
items.cta: Get a Porsche engine quote now

items.question: Do reconditioned Porsche engines come with warranty?
items.answer: Yes, every reconditioned Porsche engine supplied through EngineMarket carries a minimum 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty, with extended 24‑month coverage often available on fully rebuilt or remanufactured units. This warranty covers major internals including the crankshaft, pistons, timing chain, and oil pump against manufacturing defects. We always advise confirming exact terms directly with your chosen supplier, as coverage for ancillaries like turbos or DFI components can vary. Find reconditioned Porsche engines with comprehensive warranty cover for complete peace of mind.

items.question: Should I choose a used or reconditioned Porsche engine?
items.answer: A used Porsche engine costs less upfront (£2,500–£6,000) and suits temporary fixes or imminent vehicle sales, but carries unknown mileage and no warranty beyond the seller's guarantee. A reconditioned Porsche engine (£6,000–£15,000) includes new bearings, seals, and gaskets, plus a 12‑month warranty, and should reliably deliver another 100,000+ miles. For failure‑prone families like M96/M97 or MA1.02, we strongly recommend reconditioned units with upgraded IMS or timing components. Compare used vs recon Porsche engine options to weigh upfront cost against long-term reliability.
items.cta: Compare used and reconditioned Porsche engine options

items.question: Where can I find Porsche engine specialists near me?
items.answer: EngineMarket connects you with a UK‑wide network of certified Porsche engine specialists, with established hubs in the Midlands, South East, and North West known for flat-six and V8 expertise. Entering your registration instantly matches you with nearby suppliers who stock your specific engine code — whether M97.01 for a 997 Carrera or 9A2 for a Panamera — and offer fitting services. Nationwide insured delivery is available if no local specialist holds your required unit. Use our free comparison tool to find Porsche engine specialists near you with verified availability.
items.cta: Get quotes from UK Porsche engine specialists

items.question: What are the most common Porsche engine failures?
items.answer: The most frequent catastrophic Porsche engine failures include IMS bearing degradation on M96/M97 flat-six units (996/997.1 Boxster/Cayman), cylinder bore scoring on early Nikasil-lined blocks, timing chain tensioner wear on MA1.02 V8s (Cayenne/Panamera), turbocharger seal failure on 9A1/9A2 twin-turbo engines, and DFI carbon buildup restricting intake valves. These issues rarely allow preventative repair once symptoms appear, making full replacement the most common outcome. Get quotes for rebuilt Porsche engines with upgraded components designed to address these specific failure points.
items.cta: Get a Porsche engine quote now

items.question: How can I get the best price on a Porsche engine?
items.answer: Comparing multiple quotes is essential — prices for the same Porsche engine code can vary by £1,000+ across our specialist network. Consider a used unit for tight budgets or short-term ownership, but opt for reconditioned with upgraded components if you plan long-term use. Ask about supply-only pricing if you have a trusted independent garage, and always verify whether fitting, delivery, VAT, and old engine disposal are included in the quoted price. Use our free comparison tool to see whether a rebuilt or used Porsche engine makes financial sense for your situation.
